an10.60:0.2: 6. The Chapter on One's Own Mind
an10.60:1.1: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika's park.
an10.60:1.2: Now at that time the venerable Girimānanda was afflicted, suffering, gravely ill.
an10.60:1.3: Then the venerable Ānanda approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached and paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the venerable Ānanda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an10.60:10.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of cessation?
an10.60:10.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:10.3: 'This is peaceful, this is excellent — namely, the settling [*samatha*] of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an10.60:10.4: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of cessation.
an10.60:11.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world?
an10.60:11.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* dwells abandoning, not taking up, the *upādānas*, resolutions, fixations, and underlying tendencies of the mind [*citta*] in the world.
an10.60:11.3: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world.
an10.60:12.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*?
an10.60:12.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* is troubled by, ashamed of, and disgusted with all *saṅkhāras*.
an10.60:12.3: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*.
an10.60:13.1: And what, Ānanda, is mindfulness of breathing?
an10.60:13.10: 'Experiencing the mental *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Experiencing the mental *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.11: 'Calming the mental *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Calming the mental *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.12: 'Experiencing the mind [*citta*], I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Experiencing the mind [*citta*], I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.19: 'Contemplating relinquishing, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Contemplating relinquishing, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, sits down, having folded his legs crosswise, set his body erect, and established *sati* in front of him.
an10.60:13.20: This, Ānanda, is called mindfulness of breathing.
an10.60:13.3: With *sati* he breathes in, with *sati* he breathes out.
an10.60:13.4: Breathing in long, he knows: 'I breathe in long.' Breathing out long, he knows: 'I breathe out long.'
an10.60:13.5: Breathing in short, he knows: 'I breathe in short.' Breathing out short, he knows: 'I breathe out short.'
an10.60:13.6: 'Experiencing the whole body, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Experiencing the whole body, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.7: 'Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.8: 'Experiencing *pīti*, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Experiencing *pīti*, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:13.9: 'Experiencing *sukha*, I shall breathe in,' he trains. 'Experiencing *sukha*, I shall breathe out,' he trains.
an10.60:14.1: If you, Ānanda, were to recite to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda these ten *saññās*, there is the possibility that, upon hearing these ten *saññās*, the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda's illness would immediately subside."
an10.60:15.1: Then the venerable Ānanda, having learned these ten *saññās* in the presence of the *Bhagavā*, approached the venerable Girimānanda; having approached, he recited to the venerable Girimānanda these ten *saññās*.
an10.60:15.2: Then, upon hearing the ten *saññās*, the venerable Girimānanda's illness immediately subsided.
an10.60:15.3: And the venerable Girimānanda rose from that illness. In just this way, that illness of the venerable Girimānanda was abandoned.
an10.60:15.5: The Chapter on One's Own Mind, the first.
an10.60:16.4: roots, the one gone forth, Girī.
an10.60:2.1: "*Bhante*, the venerable Girimānanda is afflicted, suffering, gravely ill.
an10.60:2.2: It would be good, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā* were to visit the venerable Girimānanda out of compassion."
an10.60:2.3: "If you, Ānanda, were to recite to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda the ten *saññās*, there is the possibility that, upon hearing the ten *saññās*, the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda's illness would immediately subside.
an10.60:3.2: The *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccā*], the *saññā* of not-self [*anattā*], the *saññā* of foulness, the *saññā* of danger, the *saññā* of abandoning, the *saññā* of dispassion, the *saññā* of cessation, the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world, the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*, and mindfulness of breathing.
an10.60:4.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccā*]?
an10.60:4.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:4.3: 'Form [*rūpa*] is impermanent [*aniccā*], *vedanā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saññā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], *viññāṇa* is impermanent [*aniccā*].'
an10.60:4.4: Thus he dwells contemplating impermanence [*aniccā*] in these five aggregates of *upādāna*.
an10.60:4.5: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccā*].
an10.60:5.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of not-self [*anattā*]?
an10.60:5.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:5.3: 'The eye is not-self [*anattā*], forms [*rūpa*] are not-self [*anattā*], the ear is not-self [*anattā*], sounds are not-self [*anattā*], the nose is not-self [*anattā*], smells are not-self [*anattā*], the tongue is not-self [*anattā*], tastes are not-self [*anattā*], the body is not-self [*anattā*], tangibles are not-self [*anattā*], the mind [*citta*] is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].'
an10.60:5.4: Thus he dwells contemplating not-self [*anattā*] in these six internal and external dimensions [*āyatana*].
an10.60:5.5: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of not-self [*anattā*].
an10.60:6.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of foulness?
an10.60:6.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, from the soles of the feet upward and from the tips of the hair downward, enclosed by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
an10.60:6.3: 'In this body there are: head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin; flesh, sinews, bones, bone-marrow, kidneys; heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs; intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces; bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat; tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, urine.'
an10.60:6.4: Thus he dwells contemplating foulness in this body.
an10.60:6.5: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of foulness.
an10.60:7.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of danger?
an10.60:7.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:7.3: 'This body is full of *dukkha*, full of danger. Various afflictions arise in this body, namely:
an10.60:7.4: eye-disease, ear-disease, nose-disease, tongue-disease, body-disease, head-disease, ear-ache, mouth-disease, tooth-disease, lip-disease, cough, asthma, catarrh, fever, old age, stomach ailment, fainting, dysentery, colic, cholera, leprosy, boils, skin-disease, consumption, epilepsy, ringworm, itch, scab, scurvy, mange, hemorrhage, jaundice, diabetes, hemorrhoids, carbuncles, fistula; ailments arising from bile, ailments arising from phlegm, ailments arising from wind, ailments arising from their combination, ailments arising from change of season, ailments arising from irregular conduct, ailments arising from assault, ailments arising from the ripening of *kamma*; cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, urination.'
an10.60:7.5: Thus he dwells contemplating danger in this body.
an10.60:7.6: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of danger.
an10.60:8.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of abandoning?
an10.60:8.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* does not tolerate an arisen thought [*vitakka*] of sensual desire; he abandons it, dispels it, eliminates it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate an arisen thought [*vitakka*] of ill-will; he abandons it, dispels it, eliminates it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate an arisen thought [*vitakka*] of cruelty; he abandons it, dispels it, eliminates it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate any arisen evil, unwholesome *dhammas*; he abandons them, dispels them, eliminates them, and brings them to non-existence.
an10.60:8.3: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of abandoning.
an10.60:9.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of dispassion?
an10.60:9.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* who has gone to the forest, to the foot of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:9.3: 'This is peaceful, this is excellent — namely, the settling [*samatha*] of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, *nibbāna*.'
an10.60:9.4: This, Ānanda, is called the *saññā* of dispassion.
